coverage_limits: The supplied rates are synthetic observations from load-run-7.
No arrival-duration, queue-depth trace, service-time distribution, queue discipline, topology, admission policy, or scaling policy was supplied.
The long-run instability calculation is conditional on the observed rates persisting at the stated system boundary.
The logical refutation requires no long-run assumption: constant per-request work alone is insufficient to establish system scalability.
No external sources or unsupplied fixture material were consulted.

rigor.trigger: A non-trivial architecture and complexity claim asserts system scalability from per-request asymptotic cost alone.
rigor.tier_reason: The bounded synthetic fixture requires a formal counterexample and queue-capacity derivation, but it is not a safety-critical or irreversible production decision.

decision_frame.question: Does O(1) local handler work, by itself, establish that the system is scalable when observed arrivals are 140 requests/s and sustainable service capacity is 100 requests/s?
decision_frame.system_boundary: The request handler and its stated sustainable service bottleneck during load-run-7.

alternatives.description: Call the system scalable solely because local work is O(1) per request.
Treat per-request complexity as insufficient and require workload-capacity stability or scaling evidence.

hard_constraints: Local handler work is O(1) in request input size.
Only the supplied scenario and artifacts may supply empirical facts.
authorized_objectives: Assess c1 without expanding into a redesign.
Distinguish local algorithmic complexity from aggregate capacity and queue stability.
Avoid promoting post-hoc synthetic observations into stronger production evidence.
decision_frame.priority_rule: Reject a scalability conclusion if its stated premise is not sufficient; use observed rates only at the strength supported by the artifact.
assumptions: Arrival and service rates use the same request population and system boundary.
Sustainable service rate is an upper bound of 100 completed requests/s for the modeled stage while the stated configuration holds.
Each admitted request requires at least one service action.
Queue-balance conclusions apply only for intervals over which the stated rates persist.
decision_frame.uncertainty_posture: The insufficiency of O(1) as a sole scalability proof is established deductively. The magnitude and duration of runtime backlog growth remain conditional because the supplied observation contains rates but no duration or queue trace.

coverage.reason: No relation, dependency, key, decomposition, or data anomaly is at issue.
No transaction schedule, conflicting data operation, isolation level, or recoverability claim is supplied.
No partition, replica, quorum, consensus, or consistency guarantee is involved.
The claim conflates O(1) work per request with bounded aggregate work and sufficient throughput.
Scalability requires capacity/stability preconditions that the O(1) premise does not establish.
No entropy, encoding, collision, or cardinality-estimation decision is present.
A local component cost is being used to assert a system-level architecture property.
The supplied current-v1 protocol defines only seven numbered lenses and provides no P8 mapping.
The supplied current-v1 protocol defines only seven numbered lenses and provides no P9 mapping.

derivations.construct: Traffic intensity ρ=λ/μ and the necessary stability condition λ<μ for a fixed-capacity work-conserving service stage

primary_theory: Queue-balance identity Q(t)=Q(0)+A(t)-D(t) with nonnegative reflection

preconditions: Arrival and service rates refer to the same boundary.
The service stage cannot sustainably depart more than 100 requests/s.
The rates persist over the interval being analyzed.
steps: Set λ=140 requests/s and μ=100 requests/s.
A stable fixed-capacity queue requires ρ<1; here ρ>1.
From Q(t)=Q(0)+A(t)-D(t) and D(t)≤100t while A(t)=140t, Q(t)≥Q(0)+40t over any interval where those rates persist.
Therefore the stage has positive backlog drift rather than a stationary stable queue under the stated sustained-rate model.
derivations.result: The supplied rate pair is incompatible with queue stability at fixed capacity; it is direct counterevidence to calling the observed configuration scalable.
derivations.residual_mismatch: The fixture does not establish how long the rates persist, so it does not establish a particular eventual queue size or long-run production distribution.

derivations.construct: Per-operation versus aggregate asymptotic complexity, with request input size n and request count m as distinct parameters

model.aggregate_over_m_requests: O(m) and Ω(m) under the one-service-action precondition
model.terminal_state: converged for local per-request complexity; insufficient for capacity scalability

preconditions: O(1) describes local work as request input size n varies.
Each of m admitted requests requires at least one service action.
steps: O(1) in n bounds the local cost of one request; it does not make the cost of m requests O(1).
Aggregating an O(1) upper bound across m requests gives O(m) work.
Because each admitted request must receive at least one service action, processing m requests is Ω(m) in the unit-cost request model; thus aggregate request handling is Θ(m).
At 140 arrivals/s, demanded service actions exceed the sustainable 100 completions/s, so the constant per-request bound does not close the capacity gap.

The local algorithm has converged at constant asymptotic cost in n, but system scalability remains unproved because it is a different property measured against offered load m/t and available capacity.
derivations.result: O(1) per request is beneficial but is not a sufficient condition for scalable aggregate throughput; c1's sole premise does not entail its conclusion.
derivations.residual_mismatch: The constant factor and resource-scaling function are unspecified, but neither omission can turn O(1) alone into a proof of system scalability.

derivations.construct: Local-to-global composition boundary and a counterexample to a claimed sufficient condition

model.countermodel: O(1) local work with fixed μ=100/s and λ=140/s
model.required_invariant: admitted long-run λ must remain below sustainable aggregate μ, or excess work must be bounded/rejected

preconditions: A universal or sole-basis scalability claim is invalid if a model satisfying its premise violates the required system property.
steps: Formalize c1's rationale as the implication: O(1) local work is sufficient for system scalability.
The fixture supplies a model in which the antecedent is true: the handler has O(1) local work.
In the same model, λ>μ and the stability invariant fails while rates persist.
Therefore there exists a countermodel with a true premise and false scalability conclusion.
A single countermodel refutes sufficiency even if the empirical rates are later found not to persist indefinitely.
derivations.result: The system-level conclusion cannot be derived solely from the local complexity property; c1 is refuted.
derivations.residual_mismatch: No stronger conclusion is made about horizontal scalability under an unsupplied autoscaling or admission-control mechanism.

tests.belief: The load-run configuration is overloaded at the measured boundary.
tests.prediction: If arrivals remain near 140/s while departures remain capped near 100/s, queue depth will rise at approximately 40 requests/s after saturation.
tests.disconfirming_observation: At the same boundary and configuration, admitted throughput remains near 140/s without queue growth, loss, or hidden downstream accumulation, or sustainable departures are shown to be at least 140/s.
tests.test: Run a bounded steady-load interval while recording admitted arrivals, completed departures, rejections, and queue depth at the same boundary.
tests.result: The supplied artifact reports λ=140/s and μ=100/s but supplies no preregistered prediction, duration, or queue-depth trace.
tests.update: Treat the reported 40 requests/s capacity gap as post-hoc support for overload, not as independently preregistered long-run confirmation.

basis: O(1) is a bound in request input size, not a proof that aggregate capacity grows with offered load.
The supplied fixed-capacity model has ρ=1.4 and positive backlog drift while its rates persist.
That model is a counterexample to the asserted sole-basis implication.
conditions: A future scalability claim must specify the demand parameter, system boundary, capacity or scaling function, and a stability or bounded-overload criterion.
The observed configuration is stable only if admitted long-run arrival rate falls below sustainable aggregate service rate, or an explicitly modeled mechanism changes that boundary.
concessions: Constant local work avoids input-size-dependent handler degradation and is a useful component-level property.
The fixture does not prove indefinite production backlog growth because rate persistence was not supplied.
recovery_moves: Measure arrivals, completions, rejections, and queue depth in a preregistered bounded test.
Evaluate any supplied autoscaling, sharding, batching, or admission-control mechanism through its resulting aggregate μ and stability condition.
